Facts.text: Battle of Adrianople: Gothic rebel army defeats Roman forces under Emperor Valens, who is killed
Bulgaria is founded as a Khanate on the south bank of the Danube after the defeat of the Byzantine armies of Emperor Constantine IV near the Danube Delta
Construction of the Tower of Pisa begins, and it takes two centuries to complete
Quilon is the first Indian diocese erected by Pope John XXII, and Jordanus is appointed the first bishop
French cardinals meet in Anagni and declare the election of Pope Urban VI invalid
Henry van Cuyk becomes Bishop of Roermond
Jonas Bronck of Holland becomes the first European settler in the Bronx

Rear Admiral Robert Holmes leads a raid on the Dutch island of Terschelling, destroys 150 merchant ships in the Vlie estuary, and pillages the town of West-Terschelling, an act later known as "Holmes's Bonfire"
The Dutch under Admiral Cornelis Evertsen de Jonge recapture New York from the English; it is regained by the English in 1674
Netherlands accedes to the Covenant of Hanover, creating an alliance of Great Britain, the Electorate of Hanover, France, and the Kingdom of Prussia united against the Austro-Spanish powers
Fort William Henry in New York surrenders to French and Indigenous forces
Robert Gray's Columbia Rediviva returns to Boston after a 3-year journey as the first American ship to circumnavigate the globe
First horses arrive in Hawaii
Napoleon Bonaparteis sent to exile on Saint Helena aboard the British ship Northumberland
First US steam engine train runs from Albany to Schenectady, NY
Erie boat in Buffalo, NY, catches fire, killing 242 people
US-Canada border defined by the Webster-Ashburton Treaty
Austria and Sardinia sign a ceasefire
Hungarian Republic is crushed by Austria and Russia
Battle of Acapulco during the Mexican Liberal Uprising
Otis Tufts patents the first passenger elevator in the US
Battle of Cedar Mountain (Slaughter's Mountain), fought in Culpeper County, Virginia, results in a Confederate victory (US Civil War)
Skirmish at Calfkiller Creek, Tennessee - Union troops attack Confederate scouts
Siege of Fort Morgan, Alabama, by Union forces begins; Confederates surrender on August 23
First 44 Javanese arrive in Suriname to work on sugar plantations for 5 years
First US bowling magazine, Gut Holz, is published in New York

1st International Lawn Tennis Challenge, Boston, MA: Dwight Davis and Holcombe Ward beat Ernest Black and Herbert Roper Barrett 6-4, 6-4, 6-4 to give the US an unassailable 3-0 lead over the British Isles
34-35°N/98-99°W opens for US colonization
Libanus McLouth Todd of Rochester, New York, patents his check-writing machine, the Protectograph, designed to protect against check forgery
First Boy Scout camp concludes on Brownsea Island in Poole Harbour, southern England
Chicago resident Alva Fisher receives a US patent for an electric washing machine
German U-15 is sunk by the British cruiser HMS Birmingham
British attack at Chunuk Bair at Gallipoli during WWI
Treaty of Neuilly-sur-Seine between Bulgaria and the Allied Powers goes into effect
New York State Golf Association forms
113°F (45°C) recorded in Perryville, Tennessee (state record)
Animated character Betty Boop debuts in Max Fleischer's cartoon "Dizzy Dishes"
Percy Williams runs a world record of 10.3 seconds in the 100 m
Fourth Women's World Games (track and field competition for women) take place over three days at White City Stadium in London
Helen Stephens claims the women's sprint double at the Berlin Olympics, winning the 100 m and participating as part of the victorious American 4 x 100 m relay team
Hungarian swimmer Ferenc Csik wins the 100 m freestyle gold medal in 57.6 seconds at the Berlin Olympics, beating Japanese pair Masanori Yusa and Shigeo Arai
Korean nationals representing Japan earn medals in the marathon at the Berlin Olympics: Sohn Kee-chung wins gold and Nam Sung-yong wins bronze
Ranger (US) easily beats Endeavour II (England) in Race 4 to wrap up the 17th America's Cup yachting series 4-0; it is the final competition for 21 years
CBS radio broadcasts the debut of wartime series "Our Secret Weapon"
Vice-Admiral Gunichi Mikawa lands on Guadalcanal in the Solomon Islands
Smokey Bear debuts as the US Forest Service spokesperson for fire prevention; named after NYC firefighter Smokey Joe Martin
Twelve workers of the Dutch illegal paper "Trouw" are executed at Camp Vught, Netherlands

WWII: Port Chicago Mutiny - mass work stoppage by munition loaders at Port Chicago Naval facility concerned with conditions after a recent explosion in Port Chicago, California; 258 men are convicted of various insubordination charges, including 50 who are tried for mutiny, found guilty, and sentenced to 15 years of hard labor; exonerated in 2024
WWII: US Army 79th and 90th divisions enter Le Mans, France
First time all Major League Baseball games (8) are played at night
Italian rider Mario Ghella beats Reg Harris of Great Britain for the gold medal in the cycling sprint final at the London Olympics
The US crew blitzes the field to win the rowing eights gold medal at the London Olympics, beating silver medalists Great Britain by 10.2 seconds
Lusty Song, ridden by Delvin Miller, wins the Hambletonian in Goshen, New York
Dutch Korea volunteers win US Presidential Unit Citation
General strike against overtime conscription in Belgium
First elected Prime Minister of Jammu and Kashmir, Sheikh Abdullah, dismissed by the Indian government and later arrested
First statewide, state-supported educational TV network in Alabama
South African women demonstrate against pass laws
24th NFL Chicago All-Star Game: NY Giants 22, All-Stars 12 (75,000 attendees)
Race riot in Jacksonville, Florida
James Benton Parsons is the first African American to be nominated to a US Federal District Court
Britain's rock TV show "Ready Steady Go" premieres
NY Met pitcher Roger Craig's NL record-tying 18-game losing streak ends with a complete game 7–3 win over the Chicago Cubs at the Polo Grounds, NYC
Fire in Titan II silo in Searcy, Arkansas, kills 53 people
The Beatles release their "Yellow Submarine" single and their album "Revolver" in the US
Biafran offensive against the Nigerian Army
KYAY TV channel 39 in West Monroe, LA (IND) begins broadcasting
Manson Family commits Tate-LaBianca murders
Peruvian Airlines LANSA Flight 502 explodes, killing 101 people, including 45 US exchange students

Operation Demetrius (or Internment) is introduced in Northern Ireland, allowing suspected terrorists to be indefinitely detained without trial; security forces arrest 342 people suspected of supporting paramilitaries
There is widespread and severe rioting in Nationalist areas of Northern Ireland on the anniversary of the introduction of Internment
USSR launches Mars 7
Dodger Davey Lopes steals his record 32nd consecutive base without being caught
First NFL game in Louisiana Superdome; Houston defeats Saints 13-7
Clive Lloydscores a double century in 120 minutes, WI vs. Glamorgan
Pittsburgh Pirate John Candelaria no-hits the LA Dodgers, 2-0
USSR launches Luna 24, the last lunar flight to date from Earth
Chris Old completes a century in 37 minutes, Yorkshire vs. Warwickshire
Military-controlled government of Uruguay announces the return to civilian rule through general elections in 1981 to democratically elect a president and Congress
NHL refuses the merger of six World Hockey Association clubs
USSR performs an underground nuclear test
English seaside resort Brighton gets first British nudist beach
Belgian constitution is revised
Six English lifeguards set a relay swim record in the English Channel (7:17)
Daley Thompson of Great Britain scores 8,797 points to win the Olympic decathlon in Los Angeles, later recognized as a world record
STS 41-D vehicle moves out to the launch pad
Valerie Brisco-Hooks beats fellow American Florence Griffith to win the 200 m gold at the LA Olympics and completes the 200-400 m double
LA Rams beat Dallas Cowboys 28-27 in London (NFL expo)
The National Union of Mineworkers begins South Africa's longest wage strike
Cubs beat Mets 6-4 in their first official night game at Wrigley Field
Just one day after 8/8/88, the number 888 pops up as the winning lottery combination in New York
Russian spacecraft Soyuz TM-10 launches
Twelve Arab leaders agree to send pan-Arab forces to protect Saudi Arabia
British radio show "On the Hour" debuts on BBC Radio 4 with the first appearance of the Alan Partridge character
Cuban boxer Félix Savón wins the first of three consecutive Olympic heavyweight gold medals when he dominates Nigerian David Izonritei on points at the Barcelona Games
Future six-weight boxing world champion Oscar De La Hoya of the US defeats German Marco Rudolph on points to win the lightweight gold medal at the Barcelona Olympics
Record temperature recorded in Berlin-Dahlem: 99.3°F (37.4°C)
XXV Summer Olympic Games close at Estadi Olímpic de Montjuïc, Barcelona, Spain
King Albert II of Belgium is crowned
South Africa celebrates the first National Women's Day
Security guard Abner Louima is attacked by NYC police
Yankees break Minnesota Twins' Brad Radke's streak of 12 consecutive wins
Charles Kennedy wins the race to succeed Paddy Ashdown as the leader of the Liberal Democrats in the UK
The Diet of Japan enacts a law establishing the Hinomaru as the official national flag and "Kimi Ga Yo" as the national anthem

US wins all three medals in women's fencing sabre event at the Beijing Olympics, the first US podium sweep of a fencing event since 1904; Mariel Zagunis wins gold ahead of Sada Jacobson and Rebecca Ward
Philip Glass' Symphony No. 10 premieres with the Orchestre Français des Jeunes conducted by Dennis Russell Davies at the Grand Théâtre de Provence in Aix-en-Provence, France
The death toll from the Philippines' floods rises to 70
Eight people are killed and 24 are injured after gunmen open fire on a Sunni mosque in Quetta, Pakistan
At the Ferguson anniversary of the shooting of unarmed Michael Brown, a man is shot, and a state of emergency is declared
Chinese weightlifter Deng Wei sets a world record of 147 kg in the clean and jerk and another for a total score of 262 kg to win the women's 63 kg gold medal at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics
Hungary's Katinka Hosszú swims an Olympic record of 2:06.58 in the women's 200 m individual medley, winning her third gold medal of the Rio de Janeiro Olympics
Longest-ever hunger strike ends as Indian human rights campaigner Irom Sharmila tastes honey after 16 years
Nepal's parliament bans menstruation huts, coming into effect in August 2018
North Korea says it plans to fire rockets on US territory Guam in a continuing escalation of tension between it and the US
Argentina's Senate rejects a bill to legalize abortion in the first 14 weeks
EPL club Tottenham Hotspur fails to bring in a player during the transfer window for the first time since FIFA made the system compulsory in 2002-03
Saudi-led air coalition strikes a school bus in Saada Province, Yemen, killing 43 people, many of whom are children
Landslide due to torrential rain kills 59 in Mon State, Myanmar, with 80,000 in emergency evacuation sites
Roadrunner Records releases "We Are Not Your Kind", the sixth studio album by American heavy metal band Slipknot; it tops the album charts in more than ten countries
Brazil surpasses 100,000 COVID-19 deaths, the world's second highest, with over 3 million recorded cases
New Zealand reaches 100 days without community transmission of COVID-19
A herd of 14 elephants returns home from a 500 km (300 mile) trek across China, leading authorities to evacuate 150,000 people out of their path
Cholera outbreak in Nigeria has killed more than 800 people with 31,425 suspected cases since January 2021, according to the Nigeria Centre for Disease Control
Landmark UN IPCC climate report is a "Code Red for humanity", rise of 1.5°C now certain; catastrophic change can still be avoided if the world acts quickly
Ecuadorian opposition presidential candidate Fernando Villavicencio is assassinated at a campaign rally in Quito

MLB Phillies pitcher Michael Lorenzen throws a no-hitter in his first career complete game, a 7-0 win over the Washington Nationals, and outfielder Weston Wilson makes his MLB debut, hitting a home run in his first at-bat at Citizens Bank Park in Philadelphia
Philadelphia Phillies pitcher Michael Lorenzen no-hits Washington Nationals, 7-0 at Citizens Bank Park, Philadelphia
Wildfires on the Hawaiian island of Maui kill at least 97 people and destroy the historic town of Lahaina
A trainee female doctor is raped and murdered in a Kolkata hospital, sparking huge outrage and protests over sexual violence against women in India
Passenger plane crashes in the Brazilian city of Vinhedo, killing all 61 people on board on a flight to São Paulo
Jen Pawol (43) becomes first female to umpire an MLB baseball game when she joins the crew at Truist Park as Atlanta Braves host doubleheader against Miami Marlins
UK police carry out the largest mass detainments in London's history, arresting at least 466 people for peacefully protesting the UK's ban on the activist network Palestine Action
Caesar's Civil War:Julius Caesardecisively defeatsPompey the Greatat the Battle of Pharsalus, forcingPompeyto flee to Egypt
Clare of Assisi's rules confirmed by PopeInnocent IVfor Clare's Order of Poor Ladies
William I of Orangeis appointed Stadtholder of Holland, Zeeland, and Utrecht by KingPhilip II of Spain
Lord ProtectorOliver Cromwelldivides England into 11 districts
CaptainJames Cookreaches Cape Prince of Wales, Bering Strait
Robert Fultontests his steamboat on the River Seine in France, but it sinks
Napoleonannexes Westphalia as part of the First French Empire
Louis-Philippe formally accepts the crown of France following the abdication ofCharles X
US Barnburners (anti-slavery) party merges with Free Soil Party, nominatingMartin Van Burenfor president
American transcendentalistHenry David Thoreaupublishes "Walden"
Hector Berlioz's opera "Beatrice et Benedict" premieres in Baden-Baden, Germany
Henry Morton Stanley's party reaches Boma, Congo, after 999 days, losing half of its 228 members
Rudolf Dieselof Germany obtains patent #608,845 for his internal combustion engine, later known as the diesel engine
Edward VIIof Great Britain is crowned King of the United Kingdom and the British Dominions, succeeding his mother, Queen Victoria
Ty Cobb's mother, Amanda, kills her husband after mistaking him for a burglar
British naval officerDavid Beattyis confirmed in the rank of vice-admiral
Cincinnati Reds managerChristy Mathewsonsuspects Hal Chase of taking bribes to fix games and suspends him for "indifferent play"
Babe Ruthpinch-hits for Bobby Veach and flies out
Jesse Owensis part of the American 4 x 100 m relay team that wins gold in a world record time of 39.8 seconds at the Berlin Olympics; his fourth gold medal of the Games
Winston Churchillreaches Newfoundland for talks withFranklin D. Roosevelt

Dmitri Shostakovich's 7th Symphony, dedicated to the city of Leningrad, is performed in Leningrad by starving musicians and broadcast to German forces over loudspeakers during the siege by Nazi forces
Mahatma Gandhiand 50 others are arrested in Bombay after the passing of a "Quit India" motion and campaign by the All India Congress Committee
Bertolt Brecht's play "Life of Galileo" premieres in Zürich

MLB Philadelphia Phillies pitcherJim Bunningcontinues perfectly against the New York Mets until two outs in the 5th inning in the first match-up since throwing a perfect game in June when Joe Christopher beats out a bunt; Bunning masters the Mets for 15 innings over the two games
Yugoslav PresidentTitovisits Prague
Satchel Paigeis inducted into the Baseball Hall of Fame
Northern Irish guitarist Henry McCullough quits rock band Wings, founded byPaul McCartney
Adventure comedy film "Pee-wee's Big Adventure," starringPaul Reubens, is released
Edmonton Oilers tradeWayne Gretzkyto LA Kings for $15-$20 million
"Just to See You Smile" single released byTim McGraw(Billboard Song of the Year 1998)
Russian PresidentBoris Yeltsinfires his Prime Minister, Sergei Stepashin, and for the fourth time dismisses his entire cabinet
South African PresidentThabo Mbekiunveils the Women's Monument commemorating the role of women in the anti-apartheid struggle
US PresidentGeorge W. Bushannounces his support for federal funding of limited research on embryonic stem cells
SF Giants outfielderBarry Bondshits his 600th career home run
Jamaican sprint superstarUsain Boltwins the 200m at the London Olympics in 19.32 seconds, becoming the first to win the 100m and 200m double in consecutive Olympics
The US wins the women's Olympicfootball gold medal, beating Japan 2-1 in the final

American swimmerKatie Ledeckywins her second gold medal of the Rio de Janeiro Olympics in the women's 200 m freestyle with a time of 1:53.73, ahead of Sarah Sjöström of Sweden and AustralianEmma McKeon
American swimmerMichael Phelpsswims 1:53.36 to win the men's 200 m butterfly gold medal at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics, beating Japan's Masato Sakai by 0.04 seconds for his 20th Olympic gold medal

Disputed Belarusian presidential election sees longtime dictatorAlexander Lukashenkoofficially win 80% of the votes but unofficially lose 60-70% of the votes to main opposition candidateSviatlana Tsikhanouskaya, sparking widespread protests in Belarus and international condemnation
Tennis superstarSerena Williamsannounces her intention to retire in an interview with "Vogue"
US PresidentJoe Bidenbans American investments in key technology industries that could be used to enhance Chinese military capabilities
US drops second atomic bomb "Fat Man" on Nagasaki, Japan, destroying part of the city
Singapore separates from theFederation of Malaysiaand gains independence
Richard Nixonresigns as President of the United States, and Vice President Gerald Ford takes the oath of office to become the 38th US President

A giant inflatable chicken resembling US PresidentDonald Trumpis placed outside the US White House as a political protest; Trump is out of town at the time [Note: photo shows return visit to National Mall in October of 2018]
Arnold Fitz Thedmar, English chronicler of the mayors and sheriffs of London in 1188–1274, born in London (d. 1274, death reported 1275)
Izaak Walton, English biographer and author (The Compleat Angler), born in Stafford, England (d. 1683)
Johannes Cocceius, Dutch reformed theologist, born in Bremen (d. 1669)

Henry II, Dutch noble (Count of Nassau-Siegen, 1611-23), military officer, diplomat, and administrator (Governor of Hulst, 1645-52), born at Siegen Castle in Siegen, Duchy of Westphalia, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1652)
John Oldham, English satirical poet, born in Shipton Moyne, Gloucestershire (d. 1683)
Yevdokiya Lopukhina, Tsarina of Russia, first wife of Peter I of Russia, born in Moscow (d. 1731)
František Maxmilián Kaňka, Czech architect and builder, born in Prague (d. 1766)
Jacob Campo Weyerman, Dutch painter and writer active during the Dutch Enlightenment, born in Charleroi, Belgium (d. 1747)
Lorenzo Zavateri, Italian violinist and composer, born in Bologna, Papal States (d. 1764)
Joseph Wenzel I, Prince of Liechtenstein and general, born in Prague (d. 1772)
Placidus Cajetan von Camerloher, German composer, born in Murnau, Kingdom of Bavaria (d. 1782)
Augustus William, Prince of Prussia, born in Stadtschloss, Berlin, Prussia (d. 1758)
Francesco Cetti, Italian Jesuit priest and scientist, born in Mannheim, Electorate of the Palatinate, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1778)
Elizabeth Schuyler Hamilton, American socialite, philanthropist and wife of Founding Father Alexander Hamilton, born in Albany, Province of New York (d. 1854)
Thomas Telford, Scottish civil engineer (designed and constructed Menai Bridge, Wales in 1819-26), born in Eskdale, Dumfriesshire, Scotland (d. 1834)
Petrus de Wacker van Zon, Dutch lawyer, jurist, and writer - as Bruno Dalberg, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1818)
Michael Umlauf, Austrian violinist, composer and conductor (premiere of Beethoven's 9th), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1842)
Grand Duchess Alexandra Pavlovna of Russia, daughter of Emperor Paul I of Russia, born in Tsarskoye Selo, Russian Empire (d. 1801)
Nicolas-Charles Bochsa, French harpist, composer, forger, and bigamist, born in Montmédy, France (d. 1856)
Charles Robert Malden, British naval officer (discoverer of Malden Island, central Pacific), born in Putney, Surrey (d. 1855)
Joseph Locke, English railway and civil engineer, born in Attercliffe, Sheffield, Yorkshire (d. 1860)
William Thomas Ward, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Amelia County, Virginia (d. 1878)
Jonathan Homer Lane, American astrophysicist who mathematically analyzed the Sun as a gaseous body, born in Geneseo, New York (d. 1880)
William T. G. Morton, American dentist who first used ether as a surgical anaesthetic (HOF 1920), born in Charlton, Massachusetts (d. 1868)
Daniel M. Frost, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Duanesburg, New York (d. 1900)
Simon Goodell Griffin, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Newark, Ohio (d. 1887)
Elisha P. Ferry, American politician (Governor of Washington, 1872-80, 1889-93), born in Monroe County, Michigan Territory (d. 1895)
Elias Álvares Lobo, Brazilian composer, born in Itu, Brazil (d. 1901)
Brother André [Alfred Bessette], Canadian Roman Catholic lay brother and saint (St Joseph’s Oratory, Montreal), born in Mont-Saint-Grégoire, Quebec, Canada (d. 1937)
Xavier Mellery, Belgian painter and illustrator, born in Laken, Belgium (d. 1921)
Maria Vittoria dal Pozzo, Queen consort of Spain (1870-73), born in Paris (d. 1876)
Wilhelm Berger, German composer, pianist and conductor, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1911)
Randle Ayrton, British actor (The Manxman, Nell Gwyn), born in Chester, Cheshire, England (d. 1940)
Archduke Joseph August of Austria, Austrian field marshal, born in Alcsút, Kingdom of Hungary, Austria-Hungary (d. 1962)
Reynaldo Hahn, Venezuelan-French singer, composer, conductor, and music critic, born in Caracas, Venezuela (d. 1947)
Albert William Ketèlbey, British pianist and composer (In A Monastery Garden), born in Aston, Birmingham (d. 1959)
Gamzat Tsadasa, Avar-Russian poet, born in Tsada, Dagestan, Russian Empire (d. 1951)
George Hoyt, American Basketball Hall of Fame referee (author "The Theory and Practice of Basketball Officiating"), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1962)
(Maria) "Mary" Beekman, Flemish stage and silent screen actress (Genius Against Violence; Moderne Landhaaien), born in Amsterdam, Noord-Holland, Netherlands (d. 1957)
Abraham Schierbeek, Dutch biologist (Swammerdam), born in Leeuwarden, Netherlands (d. 1974)
Hans Oster, German major general, spy and "July 20th" plotter, born in Dresden, German Empire (d. 1945)
César Cortinas, Uruguayan composer, born in San José de Mayo, Uruguay (d. 1918)
Eino Kaila, Finnish psychologist and philosopher, born in Alajärvi, Finland (d. 1958)
Henech Kon. Polish composer and cabaret performer, born in Łódź, Kingdom of Poland (d. 1972)
Sándor Jemnitz, Hungarian composer, born in Budapest, Kingdom of Hungary, Austro-Hungarian Empire (d. 1963)
(Marcus) "Max" Tak, Dutch violinist and radio commentator, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1967)
Charles Delaney, American actor (Air Police, Lonesome Trail), born in New York City (d. 1959)
Nathaniel "Nat" Pendleton, American wrestler (Olympics 1920, Silver) and actor (The Thin Man, Reckless), born in Davenport, Iowa (d. 1967)
Ted Badcock, New Zealand cricketer (swing bowler in NZ's 1st Test matches), born in Abbottabad, India (d. 1982)
Erich Hückel, German physicist and physical chemist, born in Berlin, German Empire (d. 1980)
Jean Piaget, Swiss pioneer developmental psychologist and zoologist, born in Neuchâtel, Switzerland (d. 1980)
Leonide Massine, Russian ballet dancer and choreographer (Diaghilev Ballets Russes, 1914-20), born in Moscow, Russian Empire (d. 1979)
Ralph Wyckoff, American scientist and pioneer of X-ray crystallography, born in Geneva, New York (d. 1994)
Armand Salacrou, French playwright, born in Rouen, France (d. 1989)
Charles Farrell, American actor (7th Heaven, My Little Margie), born in Onset Bay, Massachusetts (d. 1990)
Gaby Casadesus (née Gabrielle l'Hôte), French classical pianist and teacher, born in Marseille, France (d. 1999)
Nobby Clark, English cricket fast bowler (8 Tests, 32 wickets, BB 5/98; Northamptonshire CCC), born in Elton, England (d. 1982)
Zino Francescatti, French violinist (New York Philharmonic, 1939), born in Marseille, France (d. 1991)
Tom Tyler [Vincent Markowski], American actor (Stagecoach; She Wore a Yellow Ribbon), born in Port Henry, New York (d. 1954)
Elizabeth Lane, 1st female British supreme court justice, born in Bowden, Cheshire (d. 1988)
Leo Genn, British lawyer and actor (Mourning Becomes Electra, Moby-Dick, Affair in Monte Carlo), born in London, England (d. 1978)
Dorothy Jordan, American movie actress (The Taming of the Shrew; The Cabin in the Cotton), born in Clarksville, Tennessee (d. 1988)
Berta Waterstradt, German writer and actress, born in Kattowitz, Upper Silesia, Germany (d. 1990)
Adam von Trott zu Solz, German lawyer, diplomat, opponent  of Nazi party, and July 20 plotter, born in Potsdam, German Empire (d. 1944)
John Baur, American museum director and author (American Paintings in 19th Century), born in Woodbridge, Connecticut (d. 1987)
Robert van Gulik, Dutch diplomat and writer (Judge Dee), born in Zutphen, Netherlands (d. 1967)
Eddie Futch, American boxing trainer, born in Hillsboro, Mississippi (d. 2001)
Khershed Meherhomji, Indian cricketer (Indian wicket-keeper 1936), born in Bombay, India (d. 1982)
Robert McCormick, American NBC newscaster (Current Opinion), born in Danville, Kentucky (d. 1985)
William Fowler, American astrophysicist (Nobel 1983), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1995)
Harry Mills, American baritone jazz and pop singer and vocal trumpet (The Mills Brothers - "Paper Doll", "Up A Lazy River", "The Glow-Worm"), born in Piqua, Ohio (d. 1982)
Herman Talmadge, American politician (70th Governor of Georgia), born in McRae, Georgia (d. 2002)
Ferenc Fricsay, Hungarian-Austrian conductor, born in Budapest, Kingdom of Hungary, Austro-Hungarian Empire (d. 1963)
Gordon Cullen, English architect (Townscape), born in Calverley (d. 1994)
Joe Mercer, English soccer midfielder (5 caps; Everton, Arsenal) and manager (Manchester City, Aston Villa, England 1974), born in Ellesmere Port, Cheshire (d. 1990)
Haim Alexander, Israeli composer, born in Berlin, Germany (d. 2012)
Robert Aldrich, American director and producer (Dirty Dozen), born in Cranston, Rhode Island (d. 1983)
Joop [Johannes] den Uyl, Prime Minister of the Netherlands (PVDA, 1973-77), born in Hilversum, Netherlands (d. 1987)
Ralph Houk, American baseball catcher, coach, manager and executive (World Series champion 1947, 52-53, coach, 58; mgr 1961-62; NY Yankees), born in Lawrence, Kansas (d. 2010)
Allen Hoskins, American child actor (Our Gang), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1980)
Enzo Biagi, Italian journalist and writer, born in Lizzano in Belvedere, Italy (d. 2007)
Ernest Angley, American Christian televangelist, born in Gastonia, North Carolina (d. 2021)
J. James Exon, American Democratic politician (33rd Governor of Nebraska), born in Geddes, South Dakota (d. 2005)
Philip Larkin, English poet and writer (North Ship, Jill), born in Radford, Coventry, Warwickshire, England (d. 1985)
Gerrit Kouwenaar, Dutch journalist and poet, born in Amsterdam (d. 2014)
David A. Huffman, American computer scientist, creator of Huffman coding, born in Ohio (d. 1999)
Len Sutton, American auto racer and broadcaster (Indianapolis 500, 1962 runner-up), born in Portland, Oregon (d. 2006)
Robert Heppener, Dutch composer (Hymns & Conversations for 28 Harps; Memento), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 2009)
Denis Atkinson, West Indian cricket all-rounder (22 Tests, 1 x 200, HS 219, 47 wickets; Barbados, Trinidad), born in Rockley, Christ Church, Barbados (d. 2001)

Gene Barge, American R&B and rock saxophonist, known as "Daddy G" (Gary Bonds - "Quarter to Three", Fontella Bass - “Rescue Me”, Jackie Wilson - “Your Love Keeps Lifting Me (Higher and Higher)”), born in Norfolk, Virginia (d. 2025)[1]
Michiel de Ruyter, Dutch jazz historian (Mister Jazz), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1994)
Daniel Keyes, American author (Flowers for Algernon), born in Boca Raton, Florida (d. 2014)
Robert Shaw, British actor (The Sting; Jaws; The Deep), novelist (The Sun Doctor), and playwright (The Man in the Glass Booth), born in Westhoughton, Lancashire (d. 1978)
Mick Loftus, Irish Gaelic footballer (Mayo) and executive (President Gaelic Athletic Association 1985–88), born in Kiltoom, Ireland (d. 2023)
Chuck Essegian, American MLB baseball player, born in Boston, Massachusetts
Gene "Big Daddy" Lipscomb, American NFL football defensive lineman, 1953-62 (Baltimore Colts, and two other teams), and professional wrestler, Born in Uniontown, Alabama (d. 1963)
Mário Zagallo, Brazilian soccer forward (33 caps; FIFA World Cup 1958, 62; Flamengo, Botafogo) and manager (Brazil FIFA World Cup 1970; Copa America 1997), born in Atalaia, Brazil (d. 2024)
Mark Weinberg, South African-British financier, born in South Africa
John Gomery, Canadian jurist (investigated 2004 sponsorship scandal), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2021)
Albert Quixall, English soccer inside-forward (5 caps; Sheffield Wednesday, Manchester United), born in Sheffield, England (d. 2020)
Tetsuko Kuroyanagi, Japanese television personality and children's author, born in Nogisaka, Tokyo
Cynthia Harris, American stage and screen actress (Edward & Mrs. Simpson; Mad About You - "Sylvia"), born in New York City (d. 2021)
Yves Coppens, French anthropologist (co-discovered 'Lucy' skeleton), born in Vannes, France (d. 2022)
Beverlee McKinsey (née Magruder), American actress (Another World, 1972-80 - "Iris Carrington"; Guiding Light, 1984-92 - "Alexandra Spaulding"), born in McAlester, Oklahoma (d. 2008)
George Leotsakos, Greek composer and musicologist, born in Athens, Greece (d. 2024)
Julián Javier, American baseball player, born in San Francisco de Macorís, Dominican Republic
Renato Longo, Italian cyclo-cross racer (World Cyclo-cross C'ships gold 1959, 62, 64, 65, 67), born in Vittorio Veneto, Italy (d. 2023)
Dick Anthony Williams, American actor (Our Family Honor), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2012)
James B. Maas, American social psychologist and sleep expert (Power Sleep), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 2025)
